column2: A voluntary process in which an independent organization reviews a hospital or health system against published standards and grants recognition when those standards are met.
column4: The Joint Commission accreditation matters because many insurers and Medicare require it as a condition of payment.

column2: A patient-safety event that reaches a patient and results in death, permanent harm, or severe temporary harm; reviewed by The Joint Commission to drive system change.
column4: A wrong-site surgery is a sentinel event and triggers a root-cause analysis under Joint Commission expectations.

column2: An infection-control practice applied to every patient — hand hygiene, appropriate PPE, safe injection practices, and sharps handling — regardless of diagnosis.
column4: Using a standard precaution means you wash your hands and wear gloves for blood contact with any patient, not only those with a known infection.

column2: An additional infection-control practice used when a specific route of transmission (contact, droplet, airborne) is known or suspected, layered on top of standard precautions.
column4: A transmission-based precaution for a measles patient adds an N95 respirator and a negative-pressure room to the standard precautions already in use.

column2: A disease-causing microorganism present in human blood or other potentially infectious materials, such as hepatitis B, hepatitis C, or HIV.
column4: OSHA's bloodborne pathogen standard requires employers to provide training, PPE, and a hepatitis B vaccination offer to workers with exposure risk.

column2: Gear worn to protect workers from exposure to hazards — in healthcare, gloves, gowns, masks, respirators, and eye protection.
column4: Personal protective equipment is the last line of defense; engineering controls like sharps containers should reduce exposure risk before PPE is relied on.

column2: The FDA program through which manufacturers, importers, and user facilities report device-related deaths, serious injuries, and certain malfunctions so the agency can track safety signals.
column4: A hospital that sees a recurring infusion-pump error files a medical device reporting submission so the FDA can investigate.

column2: The CDC-recommended list of vaccines, with ages and intervals, for children, adolescents, and adults; developed with the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices.
column4: The CDC immunization schedule is updated each year and is used by pediatric clinics, school nurses, and pharmacists to plan patient vaccinations.

column2: The coordinated public-health effort to detect, investigate, and contain a cluster of disease cases; CDC leads federal coordination and supports state and local partners.
column4: During an outbreak response, CDC epidemiologists work with state health departments to identify cases and cut transmission chains.

column2: A legally binding rule issued by a government agency under statutory authority; violating it can bring fines, loss of license, or other enforcement.
column4: OSHA's bloodborne pathogen regulation is enforceable in federal court; a Joint Commission standard, by contrast, is a condition of voluntary accreditation.

column2: A recommended practice issued by an authoritative body that is not itself legally binding, though it may be adopted into regulation or contractual requirements.
column4: WHO hand-hygiene guidelines are not U.S. law, but hospitals often adopt them through accreditation standards and internal policy.

column2: A committee formally designated to review and monitor biomedical research involving human subjects to protect their rights and welfare, required for most NIH-funded studies.
column4: An institutional review board reviews the consent forms and study protocol before enrollment in an NIH-funded clinical trial begins.

column2: Systematic investigation involving living individuals from whom data or specimens are obtained through interaction or identifiable records, regulated under the Common Rule.
column4: Human subjects research funded by NIH must follow Common Rule protections, including IRB oversight and informed consent.
